title: NOC 93102 – Pulping, papermaking and coating control operators | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s pulp and paper industry has reinvented itself as a leader in sustainable packaging and bio-materials. Pulping and Papermaking Control Operators (NOC 93102) are now at the forefront of this "Green Bio-economy," managing sophisticated, automated systems that convert sustainable wood fiber into high-value global exports. As the industry pivots away from traditional newsprint toward eco-friendly alternatives, the demand for tech-savvy operators remains robust.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category3 (Occupations usually requiring a college diploma or apprenticeship)Major Group93 – Central control and process operatorsSub-Major Group931 – Central control and process operators in processing and manufacturing

common_job_titles: Panel Board Operator – Pulp and Paper
duties: Operating and monitoring multi-function process control machinery from a central control room to produce pulp and paper.
Controlling the processing of wood, scrap pulp, and recyclable materials using process computers.
Analyzing instrument readings and production test samples to ensure product quality.
Coordinating with maintenance crews and field operators to ensure continuous system uptime.
2026 Update: Managing digital "twins" of the production line to predict equipment wear and optimize chemical usage.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is mandatory. A College Diploma in pulp and paper technology or chemical technology is now the preferred entry standard.
Experience: 3+ years of experience as a machine operator in the pulp and paper industry is typically required for control roles.
Certifications: WHMIS and provincial industrial safety certificates. Knowledge of ISO 14001 environmental standards is a major asset in 2026.
job_outlook: While automation reduces the total headcount per mill, the complexity of the remaining roles increases. The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) predicts balanced conditions, with job openings driven primarily by the high retirement rate of the current "Boomer" generation of operators.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by "Industry Modernization":
British Columbia: High demand in the North Coast and Nechako regions where facilities are integrating carbon-capture technologies.
Quebec & Ontario: Steady demand in the "Packaging Corridor," focused on recycled fiber and biodegradable coating processes.
New Brunswick & Newfoundland: Critical need for operators to replace a significant retiring workforce in legacy mills.
Why Work in Canada as a Pulp/Paper Operator?
Sustainable Industry Growth: Be part of Canada’s transition to a circular bio-economy.
High-Tech Environment: Mastery of Distributed Control Systems (DCS) and real-time chemical analysis software.
Strong Union Presence: Many roles offer competitive negotiated wages, comprehensive health benefits, and secure pensions.
Immigration Stability: TEER 3 roles are consistently eligible for Express Entry and rural-based PNP streams.
pathways: Express Entry (CEC/FSW): Fully eligible under TEER 3.
Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP): Look to the BC PNP Skilled Worker and the Ontario Employer Job Offer: In-Demand Skills stream.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): A high-success pathway for operators willing to work in established mill towns.
